summary: Parallel review: FAIL (1 critical, 11 warning, 15 suggestion). Compound gate: PASS

findingsList.text: 🟡 `bin/lib/extensions.mjs:156` — Per-extension truncation at 4096 chars; N extensions could add 4096*N to a prompt. Add a total cap for scalability.
🟡 `bin/lib/extensions.mjs:108` — `callHook` doesn't validate `hookName` against `VALID_HOOKS`; typos silently return `[]`. Add a guard.
🟡 `bin/lib/extensions.mjs:146` — Double `validateContext` call (in `mergePromptAppend` and again inside `callHook`). Harmless but redundant.
🟡 `examples/extensions/log-phases.mjs` — Missing. SPEC "Done When" requires a working example extension demonstrating all four hooks. File as backlog.
🟡 `bin/lib/doctor.mjs` — No extension reporting. SPEC "Done When" requires `agt doctor` to report loaded extension count. File as backlog.
🟡 `handshake.json:7` — Handshake claims "664 tests pass" but actual count is 85; inflated test counts erode trust in handshake data
🟡 `bin/lib/extensions.mjs:233` — `spawnSync` timeout hardcoded to 30s but `opts.timeoutMs` only controls the hook function's Promise.race, not the subprocess; these should be aligned
🟡 `bin/lib/doctor.mjs:232` — SPEC requires `agt doctor` reports loaded extension count; not implemented
🟡 (missing file) — SPEC requires example extension at `examples/extensions/log-phases.mjs`; file does not exist

🟡 `bin/lib/extensions.mjs:246` — `runExecuteRun` writes stdout+stderr without size cap; `MAX_ARTIFACT_BYTES` is only enforced in `runArtifactEmit`. A fast-output command could fill disk during the 30s timeout. Add `stdout.slice(0, MAX_ARTIFACT_BYTES)` before write.
🟡 `bin/lib/extensions.mjs:304` — Size check uses `desc.content.length` (char count) instead of `Buffer.byteLength()`. Multi-byte UTF-8 content can exceed the intended 10 MB limit on disk.

**No 🔴 critical or 🟡 warning findings.** All four veto categories (dead code, premature abstraction, unnecessary indirection, gold-plating) pass cleanly. The code is 321 lines, every abstraction has ≥2 call sites, every feature traces to a SPEC requirement, and the module is trivially deletable.
